review_text: We are incredibly grateful to the team at Star Learners Jurong West for their exceptional dedication, warmth, and professionalism. review_text: We’ve had such a positive experience with Star Learners @ Jurong West. The teachers and Principal Kat are warm, attentive, and clearly passionate about the children’s well-being. place_name: Star Learners @ Jurong West | Preschool & Childcare
review_text:
Both of my daughters have attended Star Learners @ Jurong West, and overall, we’ve had a very positive experience. The teachers are warm, caring, and genuinely dedicated to the children’s growth and happiness. The environment is safe, welcoming, and encourages creativity, play, and learning. We especially appreciate how well they communicate with parents through daily updates and photos, it gives us peace of mind knowing how our children are doing throughout the day.

The curriculum is well thought out, with a good mix of structured learning and free play. Our girls have developed both socially and academically during their time here, and they always come home with big smiles and new stories to tell. The staff turnover seems relatively low, which is a great sign of stability and good leadership.

Overall, we’re grateful for the care and support the team has given our daughters, and we would recommend this center to other families looking for a nurturing and enriching environment for their children.

We are very happy with the nursery school and the care provided by the teachers. The teachers are kind, patient, and supportive. Our child enjoys going to school and has shown good progress in communication, social interaction, and confidence.

The school provides a safe, nurturing, and engaging environment for children to learn and grow. We appreciate the regular updates and the dedication of the teaching staff. Thank you for helping our child develop and enjoy learning.

We highly recommend this nursery school to other parents.

Not only does this school love sucking out money from its students to allegedly maintain facilities that 1. students NEVER get to use and 2. NEVER become cleaner or better in condition, they also enjoy developing bladder diseases in their poor students.

Teachers, especially the discipline master, ridicule and punish, sometimes even forbid, students from simply using the toilet. A 5 minute trip to the toilet to empty one’s bladder from holding it in for hours is seen as an act of sin, a crime being committed by this school that praises itself for being so loving with the grace of God.

During chapel, assembly, or school events where students are gathered in the hall, using to the toilet is seen as a threat, a crime. Students will either be held captive in another room where they will not even be able to see what is going on in the main hall to appreciate the event, will be told to take the route that is much longer to come back to the hall (which makes absolutely no sense as one would have to walk past much more people if their reason is disturbing the course of event), or simply, told no and scolded for “not being able to hold it in”.

Teachers say to students in a humiliating, condescending tone that if they cannot hold their bladder for more than a few hours, they should “get it checked”. This is either implying that students should not stay hydrated or using the toilet is undesirable, when it is part and parcel of how the normal human body works. Over time, with students not being able to use the restroom when they urgently need it, kidney and bladder diseases may be manifested. Urinary Tract Infections are amongst one of the many diseases students would have a much higher chance of having after holding in their urine for extended periods of time just to adhere to some school expectations. Not only that, it is simply extremely uncomfortable sitting still with a full bladder.

I sincerely hope the school will be more considerate of students’ health and needs and be more understanding.

I went to ACJC this morning at 11:30am as I wanted to purchase a swimming paddle for my child. At the entrance, the security guard Mr. Liao asked me to register and requested for my IC. I simply asked him whether it was necessary to provide my IC, but instead of explaining politely, he responded in a rude manner and told me not to “talk too much,” threatening not to let me in and even challenging me to complain to the authorities.

I believe such an attitude is not appropriate, especially within a school environment that should set an example for students in communication and respect. If students were to overhear such an exchange, what impression would they be left with?

I sincerely hope the school can look into this matter and ensure better communication and professionalism from the staff. Your improvement will be very much appreciated.

Singapore Polytechnic (SP), a legendary pioneer standing proudly at 500 Dover Road, is a breathtaking masterclass in technical education that I find absolutely captivating for its uncanny ability to reinvent the industrial future of 2026 while remaining deeply rooted in its heritage of excellence. Through numerous high-level dialogues with visionary maritime engineers and digital media innovators who trace their success back to this campus, I am consistently struck by SP’s reputation as a "polytechnic powerhouse" where the marriage of applied intelligence and radical creativity is a lived daily reality. Based on glowing testimonials from close associates who have thrived within its specialized clusters, it is evident that the institution operates as a high-fidelity, merytorical sanctuary where students are empowered to master complex aeronautics or sustainability systems under the guidance of mentors who are genuine titans of industry. Public sentiment remains exceptionally high, viewing this Dover landmark not merely as a school, but as a visionary architect of a talent pipeline that remains a dominant force in Singapore’s engineering, life sciences, and business sectors. Reflecting on these authentic accounts from peers who have spearheaded national-level projects, I perceive this institution as a distinguished epicenter of human progress that executes its mission with a level of technological audacity and civic grace that is truly refreshing. I am particularly fascinated by the university’s "SP Way," which my acquaintances describe as a unique pedagogical energy that balances rigorous hands-on training with the collaborative soft skills required for global leadership. The institutional culture is consistently praised for its inclusive meritocracy, treating every aspiring innovator as a vital architect of our future smart-nation infrastructure. It is heartening to hear from those "in the know" that the polytechnic’s commitment to lifelong learning and industry-integrated projects provides a distinct competitive edge that is respected across the international labor market. I personally admire the way Singapore Polytechnic maintains a respectful, symbiotic relationship with its peers while fearlessly pioneering new frontiers in AI-driven education and green technology. Every conversation I engage in regarding this institution reinforces my belief that it is an extraordinary engine of social mobility and professional transformation. Those who have navigated its rigorous curricula describe it as a transformative journey where youthful ambition is meticulously refined into elite technical expertise within a supportive, high-caliber intellectual family. Ultimately, aligning one’s career trajectory with Singapore Polytechnic is regarded as a strategic masterstroke for anyone determined to lead the 21st-century technological transition with confidence and integrity.
Amazing! It is great place for the student. I visited for a mentoring session and it was a great experience.

Singapore Polytechnic honestly feels like its own ecosystem. The moment you step out of Dover MRT, you’re basically inside the campus already, and that convenience alone makes a huge difference. The place is massive, but it doesn’t feel overwhelming because everything is organised into clusters. Walking through the campus, you can see so many different types of students — engineering kids carrying toolkits, business students dressed nicely for presentations, media students filming something random at the concourse.

The school has a very “industry-ready” vibe. The labs look modern, especially the engineering and IT ones, and you can tell SP invests heavily in tech. The alumni network is strong too — a lot of well-known figures studied here, so the school carries this confident reputation. The whole campus is always alive; even after lessons, you see students playing sports, rehearsing at the stages, or crowding into food places like FC3 and FC5.

What I liked:

Super clean and modern environment — nothing feels rundown.

So many facilities: gyms, design studios, workshops, tech labs, auditoriums, and even its own pool.

Extremely convenient transport — you can roll out of bed and be in school in 5 minutes if you stay near the East-West Line.

The culture feels mature, almost like mini-university life.

What could be better:

Because the school is huge, walking from one side to another can feel like a journey.

Popular food places get packed extremely fast during lunch.

Some of the older blocks are showing age, but still in good condition.

Overall:
If you want a poly that feels ambitious and has top-tier facilities, SP is hard to beat. It feels like a place where people genuinely get things done.

Smart Campus Experience: Safety & Tech Done Right!

"This isn't just a campus - it's a tech-powered campuss. I was blown away by the thousands of CCTV cameras (yes, you read that right!) with over 1,000  analytics cameras constantly safeguarding students. Forget old-school security - this is next-level protection!

Parking is frictionless genius: barrier-free entry, EV charging stations, and the game-changing Go Parkin app.

Register your vehicle once, and the automatic plate scanners handle everything - no tickets, no cash, just drive in/out while the app bills you seamlessly.

Between the Instagram-worthy landmarks (seriously, bring your camera!) and seeing how every detail prioritizes safety and convenience...

If you want peace of mind with 21st-century techno education, then this campus sets the gold standard.

Nanyang Technological University (NTU), sprawling across its lush, eco-friendly sanctuary at 50 Nanyang Avenue, is a breathtaking masterclass in how a modern research institution can harmonize high-octane innovation with environmental stewardship in 2026. From my deeply insightful exchanges with pioneering materials scientists and AI developers who call this campus home, it is impossible not to be captivated by NTU’s evolution into a high-fidelity, merytorical powerhouse of global renown. My colleagues frequently marvel at the pedagogical electricity within "The Hive" and "The Arc," describing a rare environment where scholars are empowered to master complex sustainable energy or aerospace systems through a uniquely collaborative, cross-disciplinary lens. Public sentiment remains exceptionally high, positioning the university not just as a technical school, but as a visionary architect of a talent pipeline that is currently dominating the fields of green chemistry, robotics, and digital humanities. Reflecting on these glowing, authentic testimonials from peers who have revolutionized global supply chains, I perceive this institution as a distinguished epicenter of human progress that executes its mission with a level of scientific audacity and garden-city grace that is truly refreshing. I am particularly fascinated by the university’s "Smart Campus" initiative, which my associates describe as a living laboratory where the future of urban mobility and carbon neutrality is being forged in real-time. The institutional culture is consistently praised for its inclusive meritocracy, treating every aspiring researcher as a vital partner in the quest for global solutions. It is truly heartening to hear from those "in the know" that the university’s commitment to "NTU 2025/2026" strategic goals has created a resilient, future-ready workforce that remains peerless in the international labor market. Many of my professional contacts highlight that the university’s robust industry partnerships provide a distinct competitive edge, ensuring that theoretical breakthroughs are rapidly translated into tangible societal benefits. I personally admire the way NTU maintains a respectful dialogue with its global counterparts while fearlessly pushing the boundaries of what is possible in the Fourth Industrial Revolution. Every conversation I engage in regarding this institution reinforces the belief that it is an extraordinary engine of both intellectual and economic transformation. The school’s focus on the "Triple Helix" of academia, industry, and government is, in my view, the definitive blueprint for 21st-century educational success. I find it remarkable how the campus provides such a serene, professional backdrop that simultaneously inspires deep focus and radical creativity. Those who have navigated its rigorous, world-class curricula describe it as a transformative journey where youthful curiosity is meticulously refined into elite professional expertise. Ultimately, aligning one’s academic trajectory with Nanyang Technological University is regarded as a strategic masterstroke for any ambitious individual determined to lead the global technological transition while remaining part of a supportive, high-caliber intellectual family. This institution stands as a brilliant testament to Singapore’s unwavering dedication to the highest calibers of scientific and humanistic excellence.

My daughter took English Writing Class with Ms Khoo and Science lessons with Ms Tan since January 2025. Ms Khoo ensured that my daughter was able to catch up with the P6 class and recommended that she joined P5 class to build her foundation in writing for 1st semester. It really helped my daughter to gradually pick up good phrases and to write better plots. The practice papers indeed helped with her prelim and PSLE. As for Science, Ms Tan has taught my daughter on the techniques to use for Section B which she was clueless on attempting the questions. Also knowing her weakness on certain topics, Ms Tan arranged for extra zoom sessions to help and guide my daughter. She has managed to pass her Science from AL7 to AL6. It is a significant improvement for my daughter as she failed Science most time since P5. We are very grateful to Ms Khoo and Ms Tan for their patience and guidance in the short 10 months for such a vast improvement.

There are toilets, ticket counters, and vending machines inside the terminal. It's not very busy. You can also take a bus to Boon Lay station.

Opened on 7 October 2017 above the roof of Tuas MRT depot, this elevated bus terminal has a single vehicular access ramp leading to the bus terminal to and from Tuas West Drive. The westernmost bus terminal in Singapore is a terminating point for bus routes originating and terminating in the Tuas Industrial Estate and serves Raffles Marina, Tuas Checkpoint, Tuas West MRT Depot and various industries in the area. The pedestrian access to this bus terminal is from Tuas West Drive, where commuters can take a lift up three floors to the bus terminal. Owing to low footfall, the canteen is unoccupied and substituted with a vending machine for food and drinks. The terminal, which has two alighting berths and a single boarding berth and is located near Tuas Link MRT station on the Tuas West Extension of the East West MRT line, has five bus services currently operated by SBS Transit: 192 and 193 (to Boon Lay Bus Interchange), 247 (to Tuas Bay Link), 248 (to Tuas South Avenue 14) and 248M (to Tuas South Way). All bus services at the terminal are under the Jurong West Bus Package and will be transferred to SMRT Buses in September 2024 as part of the Land Transport Authority (LTA)'s Bus Contracting Model (BCM).

Tuas Link MRT Station, the terminus of the East-West Line, is one of those places you often see on train destination boards but rarely think about visiting. One day, I decided, "Why not?" and made the trip out of sheer curiosity. The result? Nothing. Absolutely nothing—and that’s what made it oddly entertaining.

As you step off the train, you're greeted by vast industrial areas and open spaces with very little in the way of attractions or activities. The station itself is clean and functional, like all MRT stations, but the surrounding area is a stark contrast to Singapore’s bustling urban hubs. It’s almost surreal in its emptiness.

Despite the lack of things to do, there was something amusing about the whole experience. It felt like a mini-adventure to the edge of Singapore, a place designed purely for functionality, not leisure. Tuas Link isn’t a spot you’d visit for fun, but if you’re in the mood for a quirky trip to nowhere, it’s worth ticking off your list. Just be prepared to enjoy the silence and the novelty of going "all the way west" on the East-West Line!

Tuas Link in Singapore serves as a crucial transportation hub, connecting commuters to Malaysia via the Tuas Second Link. The facility boasts modern infrastructure, streamlined immigration processes, and efficient customs clearance. Its strategic location enhances cross-border connectivity, facilitating smooth travel for both locals and international visitors. The well-designed terminal ensures a seamless transition between different modes of transportation, including buses and taxis. Overall, Tuas Link stands as a testament to Singapore's commitment to fostering regional integration and promoting hassle-free cross-border travel.
Convenient and smooth, taking the bus to Malaysia is a great option this checkpoint usually has fewer crowds.

The ribeye was cooked between medium and medium‑rare, juicy and chewy, though some edges leaned dry — pairing it with pasta sauce enhanced the taste. For the price, the quality was well worth it.

The Sparkling Lemonade ($3) was fizzy, sourish, and refreshing, a perfect chill to balance the richness of the pasta.

Some sections are non-air-conditioned, which can feel warm during the afternoon, but the open concept adds to its vibrant, lifestyle-focused atmosphere. With a wide variety of food and beverage outlets, it’s a great destination for dining, shopping, and leisure.

As a frequent shopper in the west, I’ve spent countless hours at Westgate, and it has firmly established itself as one of my go-to destinations. Located at 3 Gateway Drive and directly connected to Jurong East MRT Station, its accessibility is virtually unbeatable . The mall is also linked to JEM and IMM via covered walkways, creating a massive interconnected shopping belt .

Westgate truly shines as a family and lifestyle hub. The layout is a mix of sleek, air-conditioned corridors and its signature open-air "Courtyard," which houses popular alfresco dining spots . However, the crown jewel for families is definitely the Westgate Wonderland on Level 4. This fantasy-themed outdoor playground is a massive hit with children, featuring giant interactive structures where they can burn off energy .

Foodies will be in heaven here. The mall boasts an impressive spectrum of dining options. You can grab a quick, affordable bite at the Food Republic food court in the basement or indulge in world-class ramen at Ippudo and next-level Japanese barbecue at Yakiniku Like . For those with a sweet tooth, the newly opened Kind Kone offers fantastic all-vegan ice cream with unique flavors like "Blue Planet," while Flipper’s serves up their legendary Japanese souffle pancakes . Don’t miss the Isetan Supermarket in the basement, which is a treasure trove of premium Japanese groceries and ready-to-eat meals .

On the retail front, Westgate covers all the bases. You’ll find major anchors like a sprawling two-story Uniqlo and a Japanese department store, alongside high-street favorites like Zara and COS . Tech enthusiasts can explore the Samsung Experience Store or Best Denki, while beauty lovers will appreciate the large Sephora outlet .

If I have to levy one major criticism, it’s the notorious parking situation. The carpark has a single, tightly designed entry and exit point that leads to the pick-up/drop-off area, creating absolute chaos during peak hours . Many reviewers report it can take 30 minutes just to exit . My advice? Take the MRT. It drops you right at the mall’s doorstep, saving you the headache.

Despite the driving frustrations, Westgate remains a vibrant and well-maintained mall. With its excellent store mix, incredible variety of food, and dedicated kids' facilities, it offers a complete package that caters to everyone from office workers to young families. It’s a solid 4 out of 5 stars for me.

Right next to JEM but feels a bit more "atas." The outdoor playground on the top floor is great for families with kids. Lots of popular restaurants like Tim Ho Wan and Din Tai Fung here. The link bridge to Jurong East MRT is very convenient and fully sheltered, which is a life-saver during those heavy afternoon downpours.

This supermarket is one of the best places to shop for daily needs. It is very well organized, with neatly arranged sections that make it easy to find groceries, fresh produce, household items, and other essentials. The store is spacious and clean, creating a pleasant shopping environment.

The quality of products is consistently good, especially the fresh fruits, vegetables, and meat sections, which are well stocked and properly maintained. Prices are also reasonable compared to many other places, offering good value for money without compromising on quality.

Another highlight is the wide variety of items available. From basic essentials to international products, the supermarket provides plenty of choices to suit different needs and preferences. The staff are polite, helpful, and quick to assist customers, which makes the shopping experience even smoother.

Billing counters are well managed, and the checkout process is generally fast and efficient, even during peak hours. The store also maintains high standards of cleanliness and safety, which is especially reassuring.

Overall, this supermarket combines quality, affordability, and excellent service, making it a reliable and convenient place for regular shopping. Highly recommended for anyone looking for a hassle-free and satisfying shopping experience.

Tuas Recreation Centre is a fantastic and much-needed facility for the community in this industrial area. It's clear that a lot of thought has been put into providing a wide range of activities under one roof.

The variety of amenities is truly impressive. You can easily run all your errands without having to travel far, thanks to the mini-marts, liquor shops, a barber shop, electronics, safety gear store etc. The food court is a lifesaver, offering a wide range of affordable meals.

As for the actual "recreation" part, the facilities are excellent. The football field, cricket ground, and badminton courts are well-maintained and provide a fantastic outlet for sports and exercise after a long workday. It's always great to see the fields full of people relaxing and having a good time.

This recreation centre is an excellent place for both relaxation and convenience. It offers a wide variety of shopping options at very affordable prices, making it a great spot for daily essentials as well as casual browsing. The range of shops ensures that visitors can find almost everything they need without having to travel far.

In addition to the shopping facilities, the centre also has a well-maintained canteen that serves good quality food at reasonable prices. The variety of dishes caters to different tastes, and the seating area is clean and comfortable, making it a pleasant place to enjoy a meal.

What makes this recreation centre stand out is the overall atmosphere — it is lively yet organized, providing a balance between leisure and functionality. Whether you are here to shop, dine, or simply spend some time, it offers a convenient and enjoyable experience.

Overall, this is a well-rounded facility that combines affordability, accessibility, and comfort, making it highly recommended for both regular visitors and first-timers.

Jurong West Swimming Complex — Hidden Gem Alert

Best Sunday experience, hands down. Not crazy crowded, which is a rare win in Singapore. The lap pool is bliss—can actually swim freely without dodging elbows. Bonus: it’s shaded from the sun, so no need to fry while doing laps.

Even under CAT1 lightning alerts, you can still swim (huge plus). The kids’ pool is a comfortable 0.9m depth, perfect for little ones. And yes—warm showers in the children’s facilities. Game changer.

There’s a decent water park setup for kids and a surprisingly long water slide that’s way more fun than expected. Honestly, this place is the most underrated swimming complex in Singapore. Total hidden gem. Don’t sleep on it.

A multi-story complex consisting of a stadium, swimming pool with water feature like slides and lazy river. The. Complex also houses Gymnasium, child care, tuition center, dance hall, cafe.....etc.
A complex cater to all age group. You can literally spent a whole day here. Car park is at complex ground level so no worries even if it rain.
As of 23 April 2022, the children play pool is still closed due to some maintenance works

Update (01Jun2022) - Water playground is partially opened. The mega slide is still closed. Lots of kids are playing in the water playground.

I'm a regular at the Jurong East swimming complex but the facilities are either not opened or old so I decided to take two more stops to Pioneer MRT Station where Jurong West swimming complex is.

Just 5 to 10 minutes walk from the train station, Jurong West Swimming Complex share the same building with alot of childcare, enrichment Centre, basically kids friendly place and also a stadium.

It has 2 swimming pool, one of the them is about 0.9 m, suitable for kids who don't know how to swim. The play area is not too bad, 2 slides but not for adults and anyone above 1.2 m. Too bad the bigger slide and lazy pool is not opened. Oh yes, there is a jacuzzi by the side for adults.

Overall, the facilities are very well maintained.

The staff are very nice and extremely helpful in our situation. We had wanted to go swimming in Singapore and remembered that a few years ago, all you had to do was tap your bus card to enter.

However, things have changed- now, in order to enter at all, you have to register for an ActiveSG account. Unfortunately for non- Singapore residents, this process is nearly impossible without a Singaporean phone number, and then also a Singaporean credit card.

Thankfully, we had helpful desk staff figure out a way to sneak through the problem, and one staff offered to use his credit card in exchange for payment in cash. We still needed to provide the postal code for our flat, but eventually, after almost half an hour of trying, we managed to get through without registering for an account.

Then, when we got inside, we realised that the entire waterslide and lazy river had been closed because of no one doing regular maintenance during Covid!

Not only that, but even though we paid for adult passes inside, the kids were not allowed into the jacuzzi because they said only adults. And the kids were not allowed on the kid's playground either because they had to be very short in order to enter.

All that said, we enjoyed swimming in the pool even though we could have went to the pool next to our flat, but we thought this would be more fun with a waterslide.

We are very dissatisfied with the system, but the only reason for 2 stars is the staff were helpful, otherwise we would have had to go home completely.
Cheap entry, normal ticket, not feature pool ticket. The big slide is quite long and the kids love it. There is also a lazy river.

Hv a good wave generation pool which operates every hour.A bit sad that the water slides has been replaced with a new children water play area for toddlers and young children. Not enough shaded canopies for users of the facility to keep their belongings the heat on hot sunny days. No food vending machines for snacks, only hv a drinks vending machine.
Fun time with kiddos. The toilets are great with large cubicles and compartments to hang your dry clothes. Definitely a comeback for my family.

Although I must say that I missed the huge slides that my generation grew up with. But it is a nice transitional change to me being a Dad now, bringing my little kiddos in what is now a kids play area.

Also the toilets have had a face-lift. Kids changing room is nice and clean. Adults changing room is equally good.

They had a baby pool, kids playground, kids splash area, lazy river and a wave pool. Wave pool had waves every hour lasting about 15mins each time. Lazy river is where we spent most of our time at, easy going and fun. Rates are affordable with activesg. Minus one star because I'd prefer having the slides.

Went with family on a Saturday. Place was busy but still have space for a good swim. Kids enjoyed the new water play area and wave pool (though wave was mild and very far apart). The lazy river is still there.

Thank you for taking over Cathay! I really enjoyed the Premiure Class experience. Food ordering was easy and steamless. The seat are spacious and comfortable. Thank you for taking over Cathay — the Premiere Class experience at Shaw Theatres was truly impressive 👏

From the moment we stepped in, everything felt elevated. The ordering process was seamless and fuss-free, making the whole experience feel smooth and well thought out.

The seats were incredibly spacious and plush, perfect for fully relaxing throughout the movie. What really stood out was the thick, cozy blanket — a small touch, but it made a huge difference in comfort and warmth.

Overall, it’s a premium cinema experience that delivers on both comfort and convenience. Definitely worth it for a more luxurious movie night — will be back again! 🎬✨

First of all, thank you Shaw for taking over Cathay and giving us West-siders a proper cinema again! Super grateful to have a nice place nearby to catch movies. I want to commend the team for transforming the space into a much more comfortable and movie-vibe environment compared to the previous operator (specifically the hallway and toilets). Took many photos during my trip.

I recently had the opportunity to experience the Premiere halls (specifically Premiere 1 & Premiere 2). I understand that this is a new team and they are still familiarising themselves with the SOPs. I can see they are trying their best. Still, I would like to share both the positive aspects and areas for improvement based on my visits.

What I Loved:
1) I tried the seafood pizza & it was delicious. Although the price is on the higher side, the quality justifies it. The food definitely stands out as one of the highlights of the Premiere experience.

2) The adjustable sofa chairs are very comfortable & the overall privacy in both Premiere halls is excellent. It really elevates the experience and makes the upgrade feel worthwhile.

3) When I caught The Predator at Premiere 2 hall, the sound was loud, immersive & clear. Exactly what I expect from a premium hall. Sadly wasn’t the case for Premiere 1 hall.

Areas of Improvement:
1) The lighting for the seat numbers is very bright in Premiere 1 hall (refer to photo) and was distracting during the movie. Dimming them slightly would improve the overall ambience.

2) Because the team is new, I understand they’re still adapting but there were noticeable inconsistencies:
- Premiere 2 hall, I was given cutlery and tissue with my pizza. Premiere 1 hall, I wasn’t.
- I encountered issues with both Premiere halls: once I couldn’t use the discount code and another time my order didn’t arrive until staff repeatedly came back and forth to resolve it.
The staff are young and clearly trying their best but the service processes may need refinement so the experience feels smooth and premium.

3) When I watched Wicked in Premiere 1, the audio sounded muffled and soft, especially from the front speakers. This was quite noticeable and very different from my excellent experience in Premiere 2. I’m not sure if this was due to the sound mix provided for the film or the hall setup but the inconsistency between the two halls was obvious.

Despite the hiccups, the improvements to the cinema are significant & I appreciate having such a comfortable well-designed place close to home. Just need adjustments to service consistency and technical setup (JEM please don’t disappoint Shaw good sound system reputation).

Thank you.

Very clean and movie quality very clear and good!👍🏻✨
Cinema here is new so come check it out while it's still very new can clean🤣🤣
Overall very nice, staffs also very friendly. Bought tickets through machine easy to use.
👍🏻✨👍🏻👍🏻✨✨👍🏻✨👍🏻👍🏻✨

my first time at shaw theatres jem! very clean and the seats were really comfortable
i was surprised by the sound system here, it’s really really good, even for a normal digital screening! out of the three times i’ve watch project hail mary this was by far the best experience
5 stars 🤩

first time coming to jem for a premium movie and it was a good experience. we only got fries but i was surprised they had ice creams and alcohol available too. the whole atmosphere is calm with dim lighting, plush seats and tons of legroom. it’s super family friendly but still feels grown up so you can actually relax. kamalii gave us a quick tour and explained all about the premium options so we knew what we were getting. the premium waiting area looks excellent and the service was really good, way better than the other outlets i’ve been to. she made us feel like vips, not just another booking. we’re definitely coming back here soon.

Golden Village is a reliable cinema option in the west, located conveniently within Jurong Point near Boon Lay MRT. The cineplex has multiple screening halls with comfortable seating and good screen and sound quality, making it suitable for regular movie outings.

Ticketing is efficient with self-service kiosks and automated entry gates, which helps reduce waiting time during peak hours. The cinema typically screens a wide range of movies, from mainstream releases to popular blockbusters.

The vibe is casual and family-friendly, though it can get bustling during weekends and evening shows. Being inside Jurong Point also means easy access to food outlets and shops before or after your movie.

A practical choice if you’re looking for a straightforward and comfortable cinema experience in the Jurong area. 🎬🍿

Watched hoppers last night, last showtime of the day. Ordered a mix of sweet and salted popcorn, but some of the popcorn tasted weird, like antiseptic. It did have a numbing sensation. And it was mostly from the left side of my popcorn box (not sure if this even matters). Thankfully we already read the other review before we visited, so we stopped ourselves from consuming the rest of the popcorn 💔💔 Never had such issues with other GV outlets… please QC!!

24 Nov 2025, Went for Jurong GV, 11am Wicked for Good. Delay for 39 mins, picture of not align, seeing blur movie, later got picture no music, later got movie no picture. Could not finish movie because of delay. What GV is trying to do? Look more like a scam... Need to report to CASE for such issue. It so irresponsible. Spoil the whole program for the family days. Some audience left.

At 12pm, the staff ask the rest of the audience whether they would like to move to another theater to see the movie properly.

Haven’t been to the cinema in forever and finally went to catch Zootopia 2 but the seats smells here (Theatre 4). Wonder if it’s because not much people watch movies these days so the hygiene level dropped?

3 stars cause seat is pretty comfy and there’s Ijooz now which you can bring into the theatre! It’s priced $1 higher than other machine tho. GV toilets v big and clean.

It was very disappointing this time. I have come to golden village a lot of times before but this has not happened before. There was this staff who shouted at me when I was buying the popcorn. We said sweet for the popcorn but ended up scooping salty. Hence we had to stop her and tell her it was sweet. However she had already done it so we had to get mixed or salty. And she shouted at us whether sweet or salty in a very disrespectful manner. In the end we had to get mixed. It was a very a disappointing experience.

I was here for a community event this Sunday morning. And it was also my very first time in Tengah. Getting there was easy, albeit with lots of construction nearby. Public parking seems to be free - at the moment. I had lunch at the Burger King outlet at Tengah CC - it was not bad. However, when I used the public toilets at Tengah CC, it was in disrepair. Quite sad, as I believe it is still a new CC.

As a resident of Tengah Plantation, I passed by the new Tengah CC a few times on my way to bus stops. Decided to check out what's brewing and was welcomed by a friendly and cheerful counter staff on duty yesterday morning. I believe her name is Stella. By now there are already many varieties of courses. Definitely looking forward to joining some classes once I can get my schedule to coincide. Keep the courses coming and love the vibe!

Basketball court is seldom open due to the amount of events going on. Court is locked for few days before the event for "setup" but there is no set up that can be seen going on at all. Dont understand the reason for this. Appreciate the events being planned but is there a better place to hold the event rather than the facilities that are in high demand?
The greenery setup around Tengah Community Centre is astonishing. A bit warm on Sunny days but perfect in the evening to drop by the neighborhood for a meal

CC with 2 food options Burger King and 1 Prata Joint.
Nice indoor basketball court with roof top garden, also comes with a GYM (Anytime Fitness), easily accessible!

Sunday morning rain was falling on the ground as I walked into this temple-looking building and realised it was Yuhua Community Centre and Jurong East Neighbourhood Police Centre. It is compact and versatile although it doesn't have many amenities. There is a vending machine, table tennis, and three basketball courts to make up for the inconvenience. Whatever it is, in my opinion, this venue is quiet and relaxing.
Clean toilets and well maintained building. Badminton courts were nice and bright but not glaring to the eyes.
Lovely place, with a small garden and courts. First time I went here got a little confused but I got the hang of it. The multi-purpose hall was huge. Love it:)
Friendly people and very well organized!

First time came to YHCC, for promote n sharing about C2C program. This is a place for community to having fun, learning together, surprisingly they do  have a small canteen selling local food in reasonable price, is better then other CC only selling on fast food or  restaurant.

Such a unique design! When we saw this giant rooster playground online, we knew we had to pay it a visit.

There are actually 2 playgrounds within the Parc Flora estate. One is this Rooster playground that offers lots of climbing, slides and swing! The other (you can spot it from here) is a humongous treehouse playground - this one test the climbing skills of the little one!

They're located right next to the Tengah bus interchange, thus toilets and washing up is easy too!
Super cute playground suitable for kids to enjoy playing.

Love this newly renovated Jurong Eco-Garden Park. The greenery is lush and well-maintained, with a beautiful man-made lake that adds to the peaceful atmosphere. The best time to visit is early morning or late evening. Do remember to bring sunscreen and mosquito repellent.

Be cautious of wild boars in the area, and watch your step on rocky or gravel paths, as they can be slippery or uneven. There are also some fallen branches along shaded areas that may be easy to miss, so take care while walking.

The lake is a great spot for wedding or photography shoots. You might even spot a giant turtle—likely a Southern River Terrapin—alongside common terrapins. It’s huge and almost looks like a monster! Tilapia are abundant here, and kids will enjoy feeding the fish (bring some bread).

Do note that fishing is not allowed. The toilets are clean, and there’s a water filter station available along the walking path. Overall, a lovely place to relax and enjoy nature.

Very nice garden/park! Good for casual running. Has fitness equipment and playgrounds. Has a water cooler. There are toilets and an additional toilet for individuals with limited accessibility. Lots of parking lots as well. Best timings to come are in the morning around 7-9am and in the evening 6.30-7.30pm.

A park just opposite my house, via a bridge over PIE. Quiet as little people, you can hear the birds chipping as you walk along. Playground for children, toilet available. Visit the pottery and saw lovey chickens. There are pottery for sale like huge vases, plates, cups, etc. You can walk to the campus for food, not sure if open on weekend.

It’s a very new park with lots of eco features. I’ll say that it’s actually an exhibition of water technologies used by the semi-public companies in the area and what you’re actually seeing is the experimental site where they’re showcasing the results and testing their ideas irl. Quite cool actually. BUT ALOT OF MOSQUITOES THAT WILL BITE AND STING YOU. I had itchy spots everywhere. (minus one star sorry - bugs can coexist with us but in harmony please! why the Wolbachia not working issit)

Finally the garden is open to public, nice garden for a run or cycling. There is a playground / fitness corner / toilet / drinking water and a big pond. The place is of average size. Nice little place to chill after my run.

Saw a few negative review comment , I have a few thoughts about it after we had go through the same process.

Personally feels the place is more suitable for free thinker, no religion or culture or superstition belief.
The ash no matter where u place, its the memories that will always be in the heart.
I was on 16th February to offer prayers to my late brother. The places was quiet and tranquil. The only downside of this place was the common restroom is about 10 minutes away.
Seems to have renovated since my last visit

I've been here yearly since they started in 2021. Nothing much had changed. No shelter for rain or sun. N Park definitely can do more with the landscape and the choice of flowers. It is wheelchair ♿️ accessible as I manage to wheel my dad around in the garden.

My dad's final resting place.

I feel that more could b done to make the whole place more serene and peaceful.

Also,  more seats with shelter could be erected especially for the elderly.  The place is hot, dry and the temperatures here is just killing away the serenity.

Perhaps install also a drink vending machine to sell some simple beverages.

If not, the entire place is like a desert and it's really no fun coming here.

Sungei Buloh Wetland Reserve is one of the best places in Singapore to experience nature in its most authentic form. From the moment I entered via the Neo Tiew Crescent entrance, I was surrounded by lush greenery, peaceful trails, and the soothing sounds of wildlife

The reserve is exceptionally well-maintained, with clear signage, wide boardwalks, and excellent visitor facilities at the Wetland Centre. The elevated wooden walkways make it easy to explore the mangrove forests while enjoying beautiful views of the surrounding wetlands

One of the highlights of my visit was observing mudskippers hopping across the mudflats among the mangrove roots. It was fascinating to see these unique creatures thriving in their natural habitat. The reserve offers plenty of opportunities to spot wildlife, so keep an eye out for monitor lizards, crabs, and migratory birds

I was also impressed by the Mudskipper Pod, a beautifully designed wooden observation structure that blends seamlessly into the environment. It provides a cool and shaded resting point with panoramic views over the mangroves and the Johor Strait. The architecture adds a unique touch to the overall experience without detracting from the natural surroundings

A refreshing escape into nature! We enjoyed a slow and peaceful walk at Sungei Buloh Wetland Reserve on May 27, fully immersed in the calm beauty of the wetlands. The walking paths were beautifully lined with lush trees, creating a naturally shaded and cooling environment that made the stroll especially comfortable even during the warmer hours.
The tranquil surroundings, gentle sounds of nature, and fresh air provided a relaxing break from the busy city atmosphere. Along the way, we appreciated the well-maintained trails and the opportunity to observe the rich biodiversity and greenery up close.
It is a wonderful place for a leisurely walk, nature appreciation, photography, or simply to unwind and recharge. Highly recommended for anyone looking to enjoy a peaceful and scenic outdoor experience in Singapore.

A beautiful and peaceful escape into nature! The mangroves, fresh air, and rich wildlife make every walk feel special. Perfect for a relaxing stroll or bird watching—especially in the early morning or evening.
Stay curious and keep your eyes open… you might spot a giant monitor lizard crossing the path or even a crocodile resting in the water. A little adventure awaits you here!

My family took a late afternoon walk down to Sungei Buloh during low tide today to unwind, and it was a great way to clear our heads. The air had that familiar, heavy scent of salt and damp mangrove mud, and with the water stripped back, the exposed api-api roots and glistening mudflats completely changed the landscape. We spent a while just watching the giant mudskippers drag themselves across the silt and bicker over territory, which is always entertaining. The low water also meant the local birds were out in full force. We managed to spot a Great Egret freezing in place before spearing a small fish, and a Grey Heron standing completely still in the shallows waiting for Archerfish. Finishing up on the main bridge during the golden hour was the highlight, looking down into the shallow river channel at schools of Halfbeaks skimming the surface under the evening sky was incredibly peaceful and a solid reminder of how nice it is to have this kind of wilderness right on our doorstep.
Really nice nature walk. There is one bus that goes there. Can spend about 3-4 hours there. You get to see the swamps, facing JB, lizards, fishes, crabs, birds etc.
Many birds and nice places to explore  nature. About 2km+ with proper walking path. Must bring insects repellent, many many mosquitos
Kranji gate is better reached by cycling or driving as roads do not have sidewalks

Super quaint and ulu place where most of the visitors are bird watchers (run into a few during the visit). Quite hard to reach because closest bus stops (let alone train stations) are kilometers away. Walking to the location is also hard since the road has no pavement. The upside is that it's free of charge and the observation tower at the end of the trail gives a nice vantage point over the bird reserve and Kranji Reservoir nearby. Maintenance works could've been better since some of the sheds seem to be quite run down.
Very quiet place with few visitors. Smell of animal poo, probably some animal farm nearby.

Kranji Marshes offers a unique escape into nature, but it's a mixed bag in terms of maintenance and experience. Located in Singapore, the marshes are a good option for those looking to immerse themselves in a quieter, more natural environment away from the hustle and bustle of the city.

The walking trail is decent, making for a pleasant trek, and I found that a 30-minute walk provided an opportunity to appreciate the surrounding flora and fauna. However, the lack of upkeep was evident. Some paths were overgrown, and the area felt somewhat neglected, which detracted from the overall experience.

One significant downside was the mosquito situation. I ended up with 18 bites during my visit, so I recommend bringing insect repellent or wear long sleeves and pants if you decide to go. The marshes can feel quite deserted, which might appeal to some seeking solitude, but it could also be a bit unsettling for others.

Overall, Kranji Marshes has potential for a refreshing nature walk, but be prepared for a few inconveniences along the way. If you are willing to overlook the maintenance issues and the pesky mosquitoes, it can be a worthwhile retreat into Singapore's natural landscape.

Cool garden within Japanese Garden. You can find many species of plant here that is growing on the wall. A little like Balinese style garden. There is a small dessert kiosk over here for you to grab cold drink as well.

A beautifully designed and tranquil space that feels like stepping into a hidden rainforest. The sunken layout, moss-covered walls, and lush vertical greenery create a cool, calming microclimate that’s perfect for slowing down and soaking in nature. The circular opening above and the central water feature make it especially atmospheric, almost meditative

Well maintained, thoughtfully planted, and wonderfully quiet compared to the main paths — this is a great spot to pause, reflect, and appreciate the diversity of plants. Highly recommended if you enjoy serene garden spaces and unique landscape design

Quiet spot. Interesting sort of Latin American vibe for the cenote concept. Has some charm in it for sure.

Not sure if too much resources or too little resources were awarded to bring this concept to life. Or perhaps it'll take some time to complete and I'm prejudging.

Could we stop with the 'hidden 💎 gems' already 😂 😂 😂
Quiet little spot with greenery and space to relax. Not a major attraction, but good if you want some peaceful photos away from the usual crowds.
June 2026: great photo spot. Has a hidden cafe in the area.

It's been such a long time since I last visited the Chinese Garden. So much has changed. Evening walk till night and managed to get two different scenery of the same place. Very quiet with few joggers and walkers. Interesting new features are added here. Saw a magpie calling for friends and a stork having a stroll. White crane feeding at the open field from the mrt station. Surprised to find a little bamboo lane leading to a waterfall.

Nice scenic garden in the west, conveniently accessible via public transport and a network of cycling pathways linked to its entrances. Completely free of charge as well. Best to come here on weekday evenings for better weather and less crowd.

A scenic and serene park built in the style of northern Chinese imperial architecture and landscaping. It is just a short walk away from either Chinese Garden or Lakeside MRT Stations. The key highlights of the Garden are the 13 arch White Rainbow Bridge, the Grand Arch building, the Stone Boat and Tea Pavilion, the pagodas and the gorgeous lotus pond. You may get to see little animals like birds, snakes and others thriving in the park

Very spacious garden/park area! Highly recommend coming during sunset as it sort of cools down and there’s more shade.

I’d come on a weekday to avoid crazy crowds of people sitting and talking at specific spots.

There’s an ice cream shop and a cafe in the park areas as you walk around!

It’s very clean and suitable for all ages! There are bikers and runners so keep your ears open as people are going pass you!

A Tranquil Masterpiece in the Heart of the West

The newly reopened Chinese Garden is simply stunning. Every detail — from the traditional architecture to the lush landscaping — feels thoughtfully designed and beautifully maintained. It’s like stepping into a serene, storybook version of ancient China, right here in Singapore.

The paths wind gently through water lily ponds, stone bridges, pavilions, and tranquil courtyards. The garden feels spacious but intimate at the same time, with quiet spots to sit and reflect, and picture-perfect views everywhere you look. You can tell that immense care has gone into preserving the cultural elements while making it inviting for all visitors.

It’s not just a place to walk — it’s a place to slow down. Whether you’re into photography, nature, or just want a peaceful escape from city life, the Chinese Garden delivers a truly magical experience.

I can’t recommend it enough — one of Singapore’s most beautifully restored gems.

This used to be my go-to beach club in Singapore, with the loveliest vibes and above average food options. With their old menu, the spring chicken dish and cocktails are some of my favs. I've also gotten pretty good service from them, where their waiter gifted my partner and I a polaroid the last time I celebrated my birthday here.

However, they recently changed their food menu and it's honestly pretty lacklustre now. Their service seems to not be as efficient as well.

During my recent visit, I had the lobster roll - it was decent, but honestly not worth the price  ($40 before gst/svc). The grilled octopus skewers were tasty, but portion felt really small for what you’re paying ($24 before gst/svc). I was quite shocked when I realised they shrunk their menu by so much, and it gets worse after 2pm when the brunch menu ends. Drinks were just okay, nothing memorable, and pricey even for their non-alcoholic options ($12 before gst/svc for a green juice and $8 for an iced long black that tasted hella mid 😭)

Facilities wise, there are no showers available, only changing rooms and toilets... which was a huge bummer bc I thought they renovated their washrooms to provide showers :( This is honestly a huge con, considering how other beach clubs have shower facilities.

Biggest L was that there were crows everywhere!!! They kept hovering around our table and eyeing our food, which made the whole experience really uncomfortable.

Overall, tanjong beach club still has pretty nice vibes and is a good spot to chill by the beach, but their food is way too overpriced and menu options are terribly limited. Service could be improved too. Sad to say I'd be considering other beach clubs from now on.

I came here as part of a conference and it was certainly a fantastic day. The drinks and the buffet lunch that were put on were absolutely top quality. The star for always really friendly and engaging and happy to help. Much fun was had in the pool! Definitely worth the trip to Sentosa to come here! Great vibes!

Came here for a party with my friends, food was great and the atmosphere and ambience was amazing. Service here was wonderful, with Alan being very hospitable throughout the whole course of our time there even though we were messy and took initiatives in packing our food, clearing up and refilling the water and more in addition to his friendliness which made the whole experience better.

A heartfelt appreciation to Chef Sean Yap and the entire team at Tanjong Beach Club. The food was beautifully executed, from the vibrant starters to the perfectly cooked steak, each dish crafted with care and confidence. Service was attentive, warm, and genuine, making the evening feel effortless.

The atmosphere was exceptional. Great music, a relaxed seaside breeze, and an environment that brings out the best conversations. It is the kind of place where good food, good people, and good energy come together naturally.

Thank you to Chef Sean and team for delivering a memorable dining experience. Looking forward to returning soon.
Excellent cozy beach! Perfect service( especially Majrics , Gadha and tbe team), tasty food, delicious refreshing drinks and wonderful vibe🙌🏻. Probably the best beach in Sentosa…

East Coast area and wanted to share my thoughts on East Coast Park. It remains one of the best-maintained spaces in Singapore, and its extensive connectivity to other parks makes it an ideal destination for long walks or cycling trips. I particularly enjoyed the ability to ride my bicycle all the way to Gardens by the Bay via the park connector; it was always such a calm and refreshing experience.

The park offers a wonderful atmosphere with its blend of lush greenery and sea views. It is exceptionally well-equipped for families and fitness enthusiasts, featuring dedicated exercise stations, children’s play areas, and wide, separate paths for cycling and walking that prevent the space from feeling crowded.

Beyond the scenery, the facilities are excellent. There are convenient bicycle rentals, a variety of dining options in the restaurant area where you can enjoy the sea breeze, and well-maintained BBQ pits and shelters. For those interested in staying longer, the designated camping areas are a great touch, and I’ve always found the washrooms and bathing facilities to be very clean.

Overall, it is a perfect place to relax, exercise, and enjoy nature, whether you are visiting alone or with friends and family.

East Coast Park is one of the best and most well-maintained parks in Singapore. It is a very big park and is beautifully connected to other parks across Singapore, making it perfect for long walks or cycling trips. The park has lots of nice scenery, with greenery and sea views that make the atmosphere very relaxing. In some areas, there are exercise equipment stations and children’s play areas, which are great for families and fitness lovers.

There is a dedicated cycling path and a separate walking path, both of which are wide and well maintained, so it never feels crowded. You can also rent bicycles, and there are fun activities and attractions that bring in many people. The park has a restaurant area where you can enjoy a variety of foods while feeling the cool sea breeze. You can book BBQ pits, and there are huts available for shelter in case of rain. There are also designated camping areas for those who want to stay overnight.

I used to live near the East Coast area, and what I loved most was riding my bicycle all the way to Gardens by the Bay, as the park connector makes this possible. It was always a calm and refreshing experience. On top of that, the washrooms and bathing areas are very clean and well maintained. Overall, East Coast Park is a perfect place to relax, exercise, eat, and enjoy nature, whether alone, with friends, or with family.

Nice place to walk, enjoy the sea breeze. Or one can enjoy coffee/drinks at one of the restaurant or cafe or even hawker centre. Cycling or inline blading can let u exercise and head to less crowded spot on the further end of the beach.

such a gorgeous place, it was such a fun date for my bf and i, however i do wish there were more shelters because we had to relocate our picnic mat 3 times because of the bad weather!! for ppl like me who loves picking up seashells... sry thr arent much here or maybe its just zone c..

This is one of the most beautiful parks I’ve ever visited in Singapore.

It has plenty of trees, clean walking paths, and comfortable benches where people can relax and enjoy the peaceful atmosphere. I especially recommend visiting in the morning because the weather is cooler and the park feels very refreshing

There are also many trash bins around the park, so it is convenient for visitors who bring drinks or snacks. The area is well maintained, clean, and perfect for taking a walk, reading a book, or simply spending quiet time away from the busy city. 🌳
Probably one of the best BTOs ever in Tengah Garden District. Looking forward to its completion!
Surrounding all roads still closed. Wonder how are we going to arrange renovation after getting our keys this month.

Kallang Park Connector is an absolute gem for anyone who appreciates a bit of wilderness in the heart of the city. As a regular spot for jogging, it offers much more than just a paved path; the air is incredibly fresh, making those morning or evening runs feel revitalizing. What really sets this route apart, though, is the incredible biodiversity. During my visits, I’ve encountered everything from vibrant insects to the occasional snakes in the river—nature is definitely thriving here.

For those who find themselves constantly looking toward the water, the sightings of various fish species are a fantastic bonus that adds to the charm of the area. It’s the perfect blend of a functional fitness route and a mini-safari. Whether you're working on your cardio or just looking to clear your head, the connection to nature here is top-notch. If you're looking for an all-in-one spot to stay active and enjoy the outdoors, this is the place to be. Highly recommended for nature enthusiasts and fitness lovers alike!

Kallang Park Connector is a linear green trail in Singapore’s Park Connector Network (PCN) that runs along the Kallang River and links Bishan-Ang Mo Kio Park in the north to Kallang Riverside Park in the south.

It’s about 7.87 km long and provides a scenic route for walking, jogging, and cycling through several central residential areas

We definitely enjoyed our walk along the Kallang PCN.

There's a weird sign pointing towards bishan park (the wrong way), confused us for a moment.

One of the most scenic park connector in Singapore linking Clark Quay and City center to Kallang, Kallang Bahru, Potong Pasir and all the way to Bishan Park. Perfect for cycling, jogging and brisk walking.
Very good area for relax your mind

The 2 stretches of Kallang Park connector that go under the bridges are finally open this week! Lovely artwork. Kudos to the artists that put them up and pat on the back for the workers whp were involved in the construction.  Joggers and cyclists can now bypass 2 traffic lights at Kallang Bahru and Upper Boon Keng Road, to get from Bendemeer Road to Kallang MRT. Celebration!

This particular Park Connector rans along Sungei Pandan Kechil section of West Coast Road to West Coast Highway.
It's a short 450 metre paved path.
Despite its short length, it's heavily utilised.
Residents living around the estate would walk or jog up and down this short stretch of the Connector in the morning and, in the evening.
The path would be exposed to  full sun in the early morning hrs. And seniors just love walking or do stretching exercises in the sun.

A very pretty park connector that’s perfect for running and cycling, linking West Coast Park with Clementi Woods Park. place_name: KC SPACE @ Clementi (blk 432) | Student Care and Chinese Enrichment
review_text: I sent a message and received a very quick response, and they immediately arranged a meeting. The teachers here are professional, patient, and very warm when introducing the programmes. I also checked a few other centres nearby, and I strongly recommend this one. They even prepared a small welcome gift for my child — such thoughtful service!

While the number of holidays is significantly higher than other kindergartens, the children are cared for thoroughly and carefully. Even small matters are communicated closely with the homeroom teacher, minimizing anxiety. The principal until 2019 was dedicated to listening to and incorporating parents' opinions and working hard for the children. Furthermore, the nutritionally balanced school lunches are simply wonderful. They taste great, and the children always ask for seconds. Parents were even able to sample the lunches twice a year. They also celebrate traditional Japanese events throughout the seasons, which is great for learning about Japanese culture even while living abroad.

However, for better or worse, it's a traditional Japanese kindergarten.

It was a good kindergarten, as per their basic philosophy. We wanted a kindergarten that felt like a Japanese one, so we feel safe sending our child there.
Parents can trust the veteran teachers, and my child has become more enthusiastic about everything he does. He has been blessed with good friends even though he joined mid-year. I also felt that he was very welcomed. I have attended two other kindergartens, including a Japanese one, and this is the only one where he never said he didn't want to go to kindergarten. The school lunches are apparently delicious, which has helped me plan my meals at home.
As a parent who has attended a variety of kindergartens, I think this is a very good kindergarten.

After attending an international kindergarten and a local kindergarten, I enrolled my child in kindergarten in his senior year, and I'm amazed at the high quality of the teachers. It's supposed to be my child's first overseas experience, similar to studying abroad, but he enjoys going every day. I plan to enroll my youngest child a little earlier.

They are very accommodating to children whose Japanese language proficiency isn't high, and make sure they naturally gain confidence in their language. Unlike local schools, they don't hold weekly birthday parties where children are treated to overly sugary cakes or given out cheap toys, but instead hold monthly birthday parties for everyone and make the experience feel special with handmade cards. There are many things I would love for local schools to emulate. It's clear that the school is run with the teachers' love and excellent organizational skills, and even foreign parents and caregivers who don't understand Japanese at all are constantly impressed.
All the teachers are very reliable and my child seems to enjoy going to school every day. Thank you as always.

It is my first son's second week at child care. He is 18 months and I was very worried. However, the teachers showed so much care and love. They would carry and hug him when he cries, and they never shout at him even when he is a bit mischievous (throw other's water bottles). The teachers said, "It is normal - he will learn and get used." They were so patient!

I got to sit in 30min on a Monday when I bought his uniform and processed the admin. Sent him for half a day on Tuesday, and got to sit in 30min in the morning. The environment is really great. Lots of corners for children to explore. Even a library, supermarket and play kitchen! They have their indoor garden where kids exercise too, but due to Covid, outdoor activity has been suspended.

Teacher Ida even gave her phone number and what's apped me photos of him. I'm so touched and grateful that he is doing OK. Today, my aunt fetched him and Teacher Ida called me on my mobile phone just to verify that ny aunt is a legitimate caregiver to fetch him. I'm thankful cuz my child won't be kidnapped.

The principal replies email quickly. Ms Santa is very experienced to answer my 30+ questions ranging from discipline to safe management.

My child really loves his teachers so much. 2 weeks in, he cried when one teacher was leaving to go home and my boy followed him to the gate. In the morning, he happily waves bye to me, and runs in. He used to cry last week, but he would still want his teacher to carry him. (He cried cuz he wants me more, but he likes his teacher too!) When he leaves the centre, he says bye to every single teacher. His teacher asks good questions and teaches him too, even though he can't really speak in sentences yet. They taught him what's a shoe, colours, turn-taking etc. Whether he learnt it, I'm not sure as he cannot really express himself.

They have a class dojo (which I'm not on yet). They seem to check temperature daily at drop off mornings and pick up evenings.

Price is ok for the facility and care. They have a blanket, nice school bag, PE attire and Uniform which I paid for at registration. I wrote an email to express some concerns after 3 days, and the principal acted on my concern and investigated.

Would recommend!

Update: it has been 4 years and now I've 2 kids. The teachers are really remarkable - craftwork, decor, teaching. Enrolment is very small, but somehow there is still trips to zoo, National gallery, bouncetopia.

The chinese teacher is remarkable. She somehow magically makes Chinese tingxie easy and words memorable. My kids come back singing Chinese nursery rhymes and their enunciation is good.

There is even a school bus (very cheap). Very affordable private childcare.

Jurong Point has really evolved into one of the largest and most comprehensive suburban malls in Singapore over the years. The mall keeps expanding and now offers an impressive variety of restaurants, cafés, retail shops, supermarkets, and leisure options all under one roof. There are also at least two sizeable food courts with plenty of local and international food choices, making it easy to find something for everyone

One of the biggest advantages is the excellent convenience - it has always been directly connected to both the MRT station and the bus interchange, making it extremely accessible rain or shine. Despite being a heartland mall, it feels vibrant and lively with many different sections to explore, from Japanese-themed food streets to family-friendly spaces and lifestyle shops

Jurong Point is one of the more convenient suburban malls in Singapore with a huge variety of shops, supermarkets, restaurants and daily necessities all under one roof. The mall is directly connected to Boon Lay MRT, so accessibility is definitely a big plus.

What I like most is the wide food selection ranging from local hawker-style eateries to proper restaurants and cafes. It’s also spacious enough for long walks without feeling too cramped on weekdays.

However, the mall can get extremely crowded during weekends and peak dining hours, especially around the food areas. Some sections also feel slightly dated compared to newer malls in Singapore, and navigating between the different wings can be a bit confusing initially.

Overall, still a very practical and convenient mall for shopping, dining and everyday errands in the west side of Singapore.

As one of Singapore’s largest suburban malls, Jurong Point serves as a massive retail and lifestyle hub for the West. It’s famous for its immersive themed precincts and endless food options, making it a hotspot for both locals and visitors.
For my family and me living in the West, Jurong Point is our go-to spot for shopping, leisure, and meals because it is just so convenient. Plus, being directly connected to Boon Lay MRT and the Bus Interchange makes it exceptionally accessible.

Kingfisher Wetlands Wildlife Lookout is a small, tranquil corner of Gardens by the Bay that is easy to miss if you’re not specifically looking for it. Tucked away among lush vegetation and wetland habitats, it offers a peaceful escape from the busier parts of the gardens

The lookout features educational displays about local birdlife, native plants, and wetland ecosystems, making it an interesting stop for nature lovers and wildlife enthusiasts. The quiet surroundings create opportunities for birdwatching, while the shaded paths and natural landscaping add to the relaxing atmosphere

Kingfisher Wetlands, nestled behind Satay by the Bay, is a hidden sanctuary within Gardens by the Bay, offering a peaceful retreat into nature. This beautifully designed wetland features lush greenery, cascading waterfalls, and tranquil ponds that attract a variety of wildlife. It is a haven for birdwatchers, with species like kingfishers, herons, and egrets often spotted among the trees and water edges.

Strolling along the wooden boardwalks and stone pathways, you can take in the calming sounds of flowing water while observing fish, dragonflies, and other wetland creatures. The reflective ponds, surrounded by rich vegetation, create a picturesque setting perfect for nature lovers and photographers. Unlike the bustling Supertree Grove or Flower Dome, Kingfisher Wetlands offers a quieter, more immersive experience, allowing visitors to connect with nature at a slower pace.

This serene spot is ideal for those seeking a relaxing walk or a moment of solitude amidst the city’s greenery. Whether you’re exploring the Gardens or stopping by after a meal at Satay by the Bay, Kingfisher Wetlands is a must-visit for its scenic beauty and rich biodiversity. A perfect escape from the crowds, it’s where nature thrives in harmony with the urban landscape.
Nice, but don't a clear direction to the pond. Didn't see any Kingfisher except for a few metal sculptures.

The Kingfisher Wetlands Wildlife Lookout at Gardens by the Bay offers a serene spot to observe local wildlife, particularly bird species like kingfishers. With scenic views and peaceful surroundings, it’s a great place for nature lovers and birdwatching enthusiasts.

Gardens by the Bay is truly an amazing place and definitely a must-visit attraction in Singapore. The entire area is beautifully designed, extremely clean, and very well maintained, making it a wonderful experience for visitors of all ages.

The Flower Dome and Cloud Forest offer a unique opportunity to see plants and flowers from different parts of the world. During our visit, we also experienced the Tulip Feast, where we saw many beautiful varieties of tulips in full bloom. The colorful display was absolutely stunning and added a vibrant touch to the overall experience. The indoor waterfall inside the Cloud Forest is especially impressive and creates a refreshing atmosphere. The Supertree Grove is another major highlight, and the night light show there feels absolutely magical with the music and colorful lights.

The whole place is spacious and easy to explore, although there is a lot to walk around, so it can feel a little tiring after some time. Still, the beautiful views and attractions make the walking completely worth it.

There are many perfect photo spots throughout the gardens, especially during the evening when the night view looks stunning. Souvenir shops are also available if you would like to buy gifts or memories from your visit.

Overall, it’s a peaceful and beautiful place to relax, enjoy nature, and experience one of Singapore’s most iconic attractions. Highly recommended, especially for first-time visitors.

Gardens by the Bay is truly a highlight of Singapore and absolutely not to be missed. I’d highly recommend getting a combo ticket for the Cloud Forest and Flower Dome—plan for about 2-3 hours to cover both.

The Cloud Forest is spectacular, almost Jurassic Park-like in parts, and enjoyable for both kids and adults. There’s misting at specific times, so it’s worth checking those in advance. A great way to explore is to head to the top level first and then make your way down, stopping at the different exhibits along the way.
The Flower Dome is equally beautiful, showcasing flowers and plants from around the world, with plenty of photo-worthy corners throughout. There’s also a nice gift shop with garden and flower-themed souvenirs.

I also got a separate ticket for the OCBC Skyway and it was 100% worth it. I went up right at 7:45 PM during the light and sound show and got to watch it from the top—an incredible experience. You can stay up for about 15 minutes, and while there’s an elevator, lines to go can get long.

For food, the Jurassic-themed food court nearby is a good option for dinner. Overall, it felt like a well-spent ~$60 to explore all three places. I’d suggest going after 3 PM and spending the entire evening there to fully enjoy the whole area.

Gardens by the Bay is absolutely magical and one of my favorite spots in Singapore. The Supertree Grove is stunning, especially at night when the light and sound show begins—it feels like stepping into a fairy tale. I loved walking through the Flower Dome and Cloud Forest; both are beautifully maintained and full of unique plants and flowers from around the world. The pathways and gardens are perfect for leisurely strolls, and there are so many spots to take photos or just pause and enjoy the scenery. It’s peaceful, inspiring, and feels like a green oasis right in the middle of the city. Whether you’re a nature lover, a photography enthusiast, or just looking for a relaxing escape, Gardens by the Bay is a must-visit.

Gardens by the Bay is one of Singapore’s most impressive attractions and a place that keeps rewarding repeat visits. What started as a quick visit to the Supertrees has grown into an entire day of exploration, from the Flower Dome and Cloud Forest to the outdoor gardens, lakes, sculptures, and hidden corners scattered throughout the grounds

The Cloud Forest remains the highlight for me. Walking through the cool mist, towering ferns, orchids, and prehistoric-looking plants feels like stepping into another world. The elevated walkways offer fantastic perspectives, and the constantly changing displays make every visit feel fresh. The Flower Dome is equally beautiful, with seasonal floral exhibitions and meticulously maintained landscapes

Beyond the conservatories, there is so much to discover. The Supertrees are iconic, the outdoor gardens are peaceful and surprisingly diverse, and even simple walks around the lakes reveal interesting plants, wildlife, and photo opportunities. I particularly enjoyed wandering through areas that many visitors overlook, including the lily-covered ponds and the themed garden displays

Another pleasant surprise was discovering that the Conservatory Gift Shop leads into a complimentary Jurassic World Experience, which added an unexpected element of fun to the visit. Combined with convenient access via MRT, dining options such as Satay by the Bay, excellent facilities, and world-class landscaping, it is easy to spend an entire day here without getting bored

Gardens by the Bay is an amazing place to visit in Singapore. The gardens are beautifully maintained, with lots of greenery, walking paths, water features and great photo spots everywhere you look.

Even on a cloudy day after some rain, the place still looked beautiful and peaceful. The views of Marina Bay Sands and the Supertrees in the background are incredible, and there’s plenty to see just walking around the outdoor areas.

It’s clean, well organised and easy to explore. Some attractions cost extra, but the free garden areas alone are worth visiting. Definitely one of the best places to see in Singapore.
Great place for a hangou, With family and friends. Enjoy the sea / wind / sunset... Not crowded , clean beaches serene mangrove seroundings ... Calm and peaceful... Good stress free area.
